title: Switzerland’s 10 Million Question
gist: Paywall teaser: Cedric Gemehl frames Switzerland as the macroeconomic ideal Western policymakers dream of — frequent budget surpluses, falling public debt, a large industrial base, contained inflation, strong equity returns and a steadily appreciating currency — and attributes it to direct democracy, before posing a '10 million' (population) question. Light card — intro only.

insights.claim: Over the past 25 years Switzerland ran budget surpluses in 16 years and cut its public debt burden from 52% to 39% of GDP, with industry consistently above 20% of output and a persistent, expanding trade surplus.
insights.speaker: Cedric Gemehl
insights.so_what: Quantifies the fiscal/industrial strength underpinning Swiss-asset resilience — context for CHF and Swiss equity allocation.
insights.quote: over the past 25 years has recorded budget surpluses in 16 years, while reducing its public debt burden from 52% to 39% of GDP

insights.claim: Over the same 25 years Swiss inflation never exceeded 3.3% (even in the 2022 global inflation shock), the equity market returned an average 9.2% a year, and the currency appreciated by an average 2.7% a year.
insights.speaker: Cedric Gemehl
insights.so_what: Rare combination of low inflation, equity returns and currency appreciation — the empirical case for CHF as a structural store of value.
insights.quote: its equity market delivered an average annual return of 9.2%, while its currency appreciated by an average of 2.7% a year

insights.claim: Switzerland's macroeconomic success is attributed to its system of direct democracy, which gives citizens regular veto and agenda-setting power over public policy through referendums and popular initiatives.
insights.speaker: Cedric Gemehl
insights.so_what: Frames institutional design as the driver of fiscal discipline — a lens for assessing policy credibility elsewhere.
insights.quote: That is the beauty of the Alpine nation’s system of direct democracy, which gives citizens regular veto and agenda-setting power over public policy
